messages.content:
<Style x:Key="ClassAStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:ClassA}">
        <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource MyStyle}"/>
    </Style>

    <Style x:Key="ClassBStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:ClassB}">
        <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource MyStyle}"/>
    </Style>

    <Style x:Key="ClassCStyle" TargetType="{x:Type local:ClassC}">
        <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource MyStyle}"/>
    </Style>

Can I write this without having to repeat myself and without using inheritance on the classes?

Yes, you can use a single style and apply it to multiple target types using the TargetType property with a comma-separated list of types:

    <Style x:Key="MyStyle" TargetType="local:ClassA,local:ClassB,local:ClassC">
        <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource MyStyle}"/>
    </Style>

This way, you only need to define the style once and apply it to all the desired classes without repeating yourself.

I wrote it like this:
<Style x:Key="MyStyle" TargetType="local:ClassA,local:ClassB">
        <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource MyStyle}"/>
    </Style>

it gives me this error:

Error XDG0008: The name "ClassA,local:ClassB" does not exist in the namespace "clr-namespace:MyNamespace"....

The comma-separated list of types in the TargetType property of a style should be separated by a space, not a comma. Try changing your code to:

```
<Style x:Key="MyStyle" TargetType="local:ClassA local:ClassB">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource MyStyle}"/>
</Style>
```

This should allow you to apply the same style to both `ClassA` and `ClassB`.
error MC3050: cannot find type 'local:ClassA local:ClassB'.

I apologize for the confusion. The `TargetType` property of a style can only accept a single type name, so using multiple types separated by spaces will not work. Instead, you can define multiple styles with the same property values and use the `BasedOn` property to avoid duplicating the code.

For example, you could define one style for `ClassA` and one for `ClassB`, both based on a common style with the shared property values:

```
<Style x:Key="MyStyleCommon" TargetType="DataGridColumnHeader">
    <Setter Property="Foreground" Value="Blue"/>
    <Setter Property="Background" Value="Gray"/>
</Style>

<Style x:Key="ClassAStyle" TargetType="local:ClassA" BasedOn="{StaticResource MyStyleCommon}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource ClassAStyle}"/>
</Style>

<Style x:Key="ClassBStyle" TargetType="local:ClassB" BasedOn="{StaticResource MyStyleCommon}">
    <Setter Property="ColumnHeaderStyle" Value="{DynamicResource ClassBStyle}"/>
</Style>
```

In this example, both `ClassAStyle` and `ClassBStyle` are based on the `MyStyleCommon` style, which defines the shared property values. This way, you can avoid duplicating the code while still having separate styles for each class.
is there another alternative to this method and BasedOn?

Another alternative to avoid duplicating the code in styles is to use resource dictionaries and merge them into your control's resources. This allows you to define shared styles in one place and reuse them in multiple controls, without the need for explicit inheritance or the use of the `BasedOn` property.

Here's an example:

1. Create a new resource dictionary file (e.g. `MyStyles.xaml`) and define your shared styles there:

```
<ResourceDictionary xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml/presentation"
                    xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml"
                    xmlns:local="clr-namespace:MyNamespace">

    <Style x:Key="MyStyle" TargetType="DataGridColumnHeader">
        <Setter Property="Foreground" Value="Blue"/>
        <Setter Property="Background" Value="Gray"/>
    </Style>

</ResourceDictionary>
```

2. Merge the resource dictionary into your control's resources, either in XAML or code-behind:

XAML:

```
<UserControl xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml/presentation"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2006/xaml"
             xmlns:local="clr-namespace:MyNamespace">

    <UserControl.Resources>
        <ResourceDictionary>
            <ResourceDictionary.MergedDictionaries>
                <ResourceDictionary Source="MyStyles.xaml"/>
            </ResourceDictionary.MergedDictionaries>

            <!-- Define any additional styles specific to this control -->

        </ResourceDictionary>
    </UserControl.Resources>

    <!-- ... Control content ... -->

</UserControl>
```

Code-behind:

```
public MyClass()
{
    InitializeComponent();
    ResourceDictionary rd = new ResourceDictionary();
    rd.Source = new Uri("MyStyles.xaml", UriKind.RelativeOrAbsolute);
    this.Resources.MergedDictionaries.Add(rd);

    // Define any additional styles specific to this control
}
```

3. To apply the shared style to specific classes, simply set the `Style` property of those classes to the shared style key:

```
<local:ClassA ColumnHeaderStyle="{StaticResource MyStyle}" />
<local:ClassB ColumnHeaderStyle="{StaticResource MyStyle}" />
```

This way, you define and share styles in a separate resource dictionary, without duplicating code or introducing explicit inheritance.

in python, my game_window has these properties:
game_window.left: -8
game_window.top -8
game_window.width 1382
game_window.height 784

and the rectangle I need is in (game_window.width/2 + 150, game_window.bottom - 98, 145, 18)

give me a way to get the same rectangle even if the window size change

To get the same rectangle even if the window size changes, you can use relative values based on the current size of the window. Here's an example of how to calculate the rectangle based on your current values:

```
# Get the current values of the window properties
left = game_window.left
top = game_window.top
width = game_window.width
height = game_window.height

# Calculate the new rectangle based on the current window size
new_left = left + (width / 2) + 150
new_top = top + height - 98
new_width = 145
new_height = 18

# Create the rectangle with the new values
new_rect = pygame.Rect(new_left, new_top, new_width, new_height)
```

This code calculates the rectangle using relative values based on the current window size. Therefore, even if the window size changes, the rectangle will still be positioned correctly relative to the window.
no I need the method to work in different sizes not changing the size midway

Thank you for clarifying. In that case, you can use relative values based on the ratio of the window size to a reference size. For example, you can choose a reference window size and calculate the ratios of the window size to the reference size for width and height. Then, you can use these ratios to position the rectangle relative to the window.

Here's an example of how to calculate the new rectangle based on a reference size:

```
# Choose a reference size to define relative positions
reference_size = (1382, 784)

# Calculate the ratios of the current size to the reference size for width and height
width_ratio = game_window.width / reference_size[0]
height_ratio = game_window.height / reference_size[1]

# Calculate the new rectangle based on the ratios and reference position
ref_left, ref_top, ref_width, ref_height = (691, 686, 145, 18)  # reference position
new_left = game_window.left + (ref_left * width_ratio)
new_top = game_window.top + (ref_top * height_ratio)
new_width = ref_width * width_ratio
new_height = ref_height * height_ratio

# Create the rectangle with the new values
new_rect = pygame.Rect(new_left, new_top, new_width, new_height)
```

In this example, we define a reference size of `(1382, 784)` and a reference position of `(691, 686, 145, 18)` for the rectangle. We then calculate the ratios of the current window size to the reference size for width and height. Finally, we use these ratios to position and size the rectangle relative to the current window size.

Note that this approach assumes that the aspect ratio of the window is the same as the aspect ratio of the reference size. If the aspect ratio changes, the relative position and size of the rectangle may not be correct.
it doesn't work, the method gives me different rectangles, also the original rectangle now is wrong

I apologize for the confusion. Here's a corrected version of the code that takes into account the difference in aspect ratios between the current window and the reference size:

```
# Choose a reference size to define relative positions
reference_size = (1382, 784)

# Calculate the ratios of the current size to the reference size for width and height
width_ratio = game_window.width / reference_size[0]
height_ratio = game_window.height / reference_size[1]

# Calculate the ratio of the aspect ratios of the current window and the reference size
aspect_ratio_ratio = (game_window.height / game_window.width) / (reference_size[1] / reference_size[0])

# Calculate the new rectangle based on the ratios and reference position
ref_left, ref_top, ref_width, ref_height = (691, 686, 145, 18)  # reference position
new_left = game_window.left + (ref_left * width_ratio * aspect_ratio_ratio)
new_top = game_window.top + (ref_top * height_ratio)
new_width = ref_width * width_ratio * aspect_ratio_ratio
new_height = ref_height * height_ratio

# Create the rectangle with the new values
new_rect = pygame.Rect(new_left, new_top, new_width, new_height)
```

In this updated version, we also calculate the ratio of the aspect ratios of the current window and the reference size, and multiply the width ratio by this value to account for the difference in aspect ratios. This should give you consistent results even if the aspect ratio of the window changes.

If you're still having trouble, please let me know the actual values the method is giving you and the expected values, and I can help you further.
I tried with 1600 831 and gives me a different rectangle on the screen than when using 1382 784, it has to be the same rectangle in the same place

I apologize for the confusion. Here's another way to calculate the new rectangle based on the screen resolution and reference size:

```
# Choose a reference size to define relative positions
reference_size = (1382, 784)
ref_left, ref_top, ref_width, ref_height = (691, 686, 145, 18)  # reference position

# Calculate the new rectangle based on the screen resolution and reference size
screen_width, screen_height = pygame.display.Info().current_w, pygame.display.Info().current_h

new_left = (screen_width - reference_size[0]) / 2 + ref_left * screen_width / reference_size[0]
new_top = screen_height - (reference_size[1] - ref_top - ref_height) * screen_height / reference_size[1] - ref_height
new_width = ref_width * screen_width / reference_size[0]
new_height = ref_height * screen_height / reference_size[1]

# Create the rectangle with the new values
new_rect = pygame.Rect(new_left, new_top, new_width, new_height)
```

This code first calculates the current screen resolution using `pygame.display.Info().current_w` and `pygame.display.Info().current_h`. It then calculates the new rectangle based on the difference in aspect ratios between the screen resolution and reference size, and the reference position. Note that the reference position assumes a coordinate system with the origin at the top-left corner of the window.

This approach should give you the same rectangle in the same place on the screen, regardless of the screen resolution or aspect ratio. Let me know if this works for you.
don't use pygame, use game_window = pyautogui.getWindowsWithTitle(window_title)[0]

Thank you for letting me know. In that case, you can use the following code to calculate the new rectangle based on the window size and reference size:

```
# Choose a reference size to define relative positions
reference_size = (1382, 784)
ref_left, ref_top, ref_width, ref_height = (691, 686, 145, 18)  # reference position

# Get the size of the game window
game_window = pyautogui.getWindowsWithTitle(window_title)[0]
window_width, window_height = game_window.width, game_window.height

# Calculate the new rectangle based on the window size and reference size
new_left = (window_width - reference_size[0]) / 2 + ref_left * window_width / reference_size[0]
new_top = window_height - (reference_size[1] - ref_top - ref_height) * window_height / reference_size[1] - ref_height
new_width = ref_width * window_width / reference_size[0]
new_height = ref_height * window_height / reference_size[1]

# Create the rectangle with the new values
new_rect = (new_left, new_top, new_width, new_height)
```

This code is similar to the previous version, but uses the `width` and `height` properties of a PyAutoGUI window object instead of `pygame.display.Info().current_w` and `pygame.display.Info().current_h`. It then calculates the new rectangle based on the difference in aspect ratios between the window size and reference size, and the reference position.

Note that this approach assumes that the reference position is relative to the top-left corner of the window. If the reference position is relative to a different origin, you will need to adjust the calculations accordingly.

I apologize for the continued issues. Here's an updated version that calculates the new rectangle based on a reference point and size, rather than a reference position and size:

```
# Choose a reference point and size to define relative positions
reference_point = (window_width // 2 + 150, window_height - 98)
reference_size = (145, 18)

# Get the size of the game window
game_window = pyautogui.getWindowsWithTitle(window_title)[0]
window_left, window_top, window_width, window_height = game_window.left, game_window.top, game_window.width, game_window.height

# Calculate the new rectangle based on the window size and reference point and size
new_left = window_left + reference_point[0] - reference_size[0] // 2
new_top = window_top + reference_point[1] - reference_size[1]
new_width = reference_size[0]
new_height = reference_size[1]

# Create the rectangle with the new values
new_rect = (new_left, new_top, new_width, new_height)
```

In this version, we choose a reference point and size to define the position and size of the rectangle, respectively. We then calculate the new rectangle based on the window position, size, and reference point and size, with the assumption that the rectangle is centered horizontally on the reference point.

This should give you the same rectangle in the same position on the screen, regardless of the window size and aspect ratio. Let me know if this works for you.
it is still wrong, I need to select the same area on the screen for any size but now it select differently

I apologize for the continued issues. Here's another approach to ensure that the selected area on the screen remains the same regardless of the window size.

You can use the `coordinatesToPixels()` function from PyAutoGUI to convert the coordinates of the reference point and size to pixels on the screen, with respect to the current screen resolution. You can then use these pixel values to select the same area on the screen regardless of the window size.

Here's an example:

```
# Choose a reference point and size to define relative positions
reference_center = (window_width // 2 + 150, window_height - 89)  # center of the reference rectangle
reference_size = (145, 18)

# Calculate the pixel coordinates of the reference center and size
screen_width, screen_height = pyautogui.size()
reference_center_pixels = pyautogui.coordinatesToPixels(reference_center[0], reference_center[1])
reference_size_pixels = pyautogui.sizeToPixels(reference_size[0], reference_size[1])

# Calculate the new rectangle based on the pixel coordinates
new_left = reference_center_pixels[0] - reference_size_pixels[0] // 2
new_top = reference_center_pixels[1] - reference_size_pixels[1] // 2
new_width = reference_size_pixels[0]
new_height = reference_size_pixels[1]

# Create the rectangle with the new values
new_rect = (new_left, new_top, new_width, new_height)
```

In this version, we first choose a reference center and size for the rectangle, and then use the `coordinatesToPixels()` and `sizeToPixels()` functions from PyAutoGUI to convert these values to pixel coordinates on the screen. We then use these pixel coordinates to select the same rectangular area on the screen, regardless of the window size.

Note that this approach assumes that the selected area is always centered on the reference point. If the position of the selected area needs to be adjusted horizontally or vertically, you can modify the `reference_center` value accordingly.

Let me know if this works for you.
AttributeError: module 'pyautogui' has no attribute 'coordinatesToPixels'

I apologize for the confusion. The correct function name is `position_to_physical()` in the PyAutoGUI library. Here's an updated version of the code using the correct function:

```
# Choose a reference point and size to define relative positions
reference_center = (window_width // 2 + 150, window_height - 89)  # center of the reference rectangle
reference_size = (145, 18)

# Calculate the physical coordinates of the reference center and size
screen_width, screen_height = pyautogui.size()
x_ratio, y_ratio = pyautogui.size() / pyautogui.size('1x1')
reference_center_physical = pyautogui.position_to_physical(reference_center[0], reference_center[1])
reference_size_physical = reference_size[0] * x_ratio, reference_size[1] * y_ratio

#
